title: How implicit/given resolution works
description: The compiler searches lexical scope then implicit scope (companion objects) for a unique matching instance, failing on ambiguity.
body: Use when reasoning about contextual abstraction. To fill an implicit/using parameter the compiler searches the local lexical scope (including imports) first, then the implicit scope: the companion objects of the types involved. It requires exactly one most-specific match; zero is a 'no implicit found' error and more than one equally-specific is an ambiguity error. Scala 3 renames implicit to given/using and makes imports of givens explicit. Do not rely on an instance being globally visible without an import unless it lives in a relevant companion. Actions: place canonical type-class instances in companions, import extras locally. Validation signals include summon/implicitly resolving the intended instance. Risk boundary: an instance silently picked from an unexpected scope changes program behavior (a different Encoder or Ordering) without any visible call-site change.
